heading: E QUANTI LI HANNO DESCRITTI
card_summary: This long chapter of the Cantico Primo catalogues the dissonance of pagan and secular philosophers in contrast with the perfect consonance of Scripture and Christian theology. Zorzi surveys the cosmological first principles proposed by Leucippus, Democritus, Thales, Anaximenes, Heraclitus, Pythagoras, Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, Epicurus, and Zeno (among many others), then chronicles competing theories of the soul from Anaximander through Plotinus, Proclus, and Averroes, and finishes with the doxography of competing views on the human final end, drawing on Augustine's report that Marcus Varro collected 288 opinions on the subject. Against this cacophony, Zorzi opposes the single harmonious choir of Hebrew prophets, evangelists, apostles, Greek and Latin Church Fathers, and Scholastic theologians — Moses, David, Paul, Augustine, Ambrose, Jerome, Thomas Aquinas, Bonaventure, Duns Scotus — whose diversely-pitched voices produce a divine harmony precisely because they all orient toward the One God as the sole source of consonance. The Hermetic tradition (Hermes Trismegistus, Zoroaster, Orpheus), Hebrew and Chaldean sages, and the Neoplatonists Plato and Plotinus occupy an intermediate position, valued for their partial alignment with divine harmony.
essay:
# E QUANTI LI HANNO DESCRITTI — Scholarly Summary (pp. 333–365)

*Note: This summary covers the opening portion of a very long section (approx. the first 6000 words / through chapter 5 of the section). Later sub-sections on pp. 350–365 are not represented in detail.*

---

## Central Argument: The One as Principle of Harmony

The overarching argument of this chapter is musical and metaphysical at once. Zorzi opens with a definition drawn from music theory — harmony is the coordination of strings and voices according to rules of consonance without violation — and deploys it as a cosmological criterion. The divergence of secular philosophers is not merely intellectual error but an acoustic catastrophe: they produce a *strepitus horribilis* that cannot be resolved into consonance. The reason, Zorzi argues by invoking Boethius, is ontological: *omne enim ideo est quia unum numero est* — everything exists insofar as it is one. Departure from the One, who is the sole giver of consonance, generates irreducible dissonance.

> «Perciò le loro dottrine (del tutto prive di ciò che conferisce a ogni cosa l'essere e la corrispondenza armoniosa), proliferando in moltitudine confusa, sono destinate a dissolversi.»

<cite>p. 333 (Italian translation, chapter 1)</cite>

---

## Chapter 1: The Dissonance of the World's Sages

Zorzi opens with a rapid doxographic survey of philosophical schools whose internal disagreements illustrate the theme. The Socratics Aristippus and Ariston disagree about the end (pleasure vs. virtue). Aristotle's followers Alexander of Aphrodisias and Averroes fracture Aristotelian psychology: Alexander posits the mortality of the soul, Averroes a single universal intellect (*intellectus unus communis*). Aristotle himself attacked his own teacher Plato at the age of twenty-one. Al-Ghazali (*Algazel*) attacks all philosophers; Averroes attacks al-Ghazali. The Pythagoreans divide over the transmigration of souls: Jarca the Brahmin and Apollonius of Tyana accept transmigration into animals; Iamblichus and Porphyry repudiate it on the grounds that the soul cannot be uprooted from its centre; Proclus, Hermias, and Syrianus find the degradation of a celestial soul into beasts horrifying; Timaeus of Locri, most noble Pythagorean, dismisses such stories as invented to terrorize men.

The pre-Socratics illustrate the same pattern on the question of the arche: Thales proposes water; Anaximander, infinite matter (*interminata materia*); Anaximenes, air; Anaxagoras, homoeomeric particles; Heraclitus and Hipparchus of Metapontum, fire; Empedocles, all four elements together; Epicurus, eternal solid corpuscles with weight added. Pythagoras alone is given credit for ascending higher than most suppose, placing the principles in formal or natural numbers — not mercantile numbers, but those known to those who have learned to philosophize and theologize through number.

> «Pitagora, spintosi a profondità maggiori di quanto credano alcuni, ritenne che i princìpi delle cose fossero creati dai numeri e dalle misure, ovvero le opportune proporzioni delle realtà soggiacenti (tali proporzioni sono dette armonie, accordi e concordanze); non ci si riferisce certo ai numeri dei quali si servono i mercanti, ma ai numeri formali o naturali.»

<cite>p. 334 (chapter 3, Italian text)</cite>

---

## Chapter 2: The Concordance of Catholic Writers

Against the philosophers' babel, Zorzi sets out an idealized polyphonic choir of sacred writers. The controlling analogy is the ancient musical scale: if all strings of a lyre sounded the same pitch (*hypate*, *perhypate*, *lichanos*, *mese*, *paramese*), there would be no harmony. Diversity of voice, coordinated toward a single sound, is necessary and good.

The choir is ordered by celestial hierarchy. John and Augustine sound as high and piercing as seraphim; Matthew and Ambrose emit grave sentential voices among the thrones; Luke and Gregory delight in cherubic sweetness; Jerome with Mark crushes heretical dissonance with the stronger voice of the powers and virtues; Solomon and Bernard sing, with the angel Gabriel, the nuptial hymns of the heavenly marriage.

Greek and Latin Scholastics are integrated into the same structure: Duns Scotus and Francis of Assisi correspond to Augustine (one with graver, more intense movement; the other swifter and more acute); Albert the Great emulates Ambrose in density of thought and investigation of the secrets of both natures; Thomas Aquinas is said to follow Bernard in sweetness and Augustinian breadth of style; Bonaventure joins Gregory in a devotion-over-doctrine modulation; Giles of Rome and Peter Aureoli recall Jerome; Henry of Ghent at the level of Origen refreshes elevated minds.

> «L'angelico Tommaso per la sua voce, per la dottrina e per la dolcezza ineffabile, segue le orme di Bernardo, per la magnificenza dello stile è degno continuatore di Agostino.»

<cite>p. 172 (chapter 2, Italian text)</cite>

The dissonant philosophers, by contrast, are likened to different species of animal — some bellowing, others neighing, bleating, roaring, barking, braying — all producing an impossible cacophony.

---

## Intermediate Tradition: Hermetica, Orphica, Hebraica

Zorzi situates several intermediate figures between pagan dissonance and Christian harmony. Hermes Trismegistus, described as Egyptian by common opinion but Midianite by others, having achieved unity with his own mind (*cum sua mente unitus*), always breathes the divine and transcends human discourse. Iamblichus, devoted to numbers and mysteries, strives to transmit Pythagorean and Egyptian sacraments. Zoroaster, Orpheus, and the Persian Charondas penetrate the secrets of nature and sound the sweet epithalamia of the natural marriage of the world. The Hebrew and Chaldean sages, instructed by divine oracles, sing a more perfect marriage — that of the archetypal pattern of the cosmic machine with the human world. This is a key moment: Zorzi grades the traditions, with Hermetic and Orphic writers capable of partial consonance, and Hebrew-Chaldean writers ascending closest to Christian harmony.

---

## Chapter 3: The Dissonance of Philosophers on First Principles

Zorzi enumerates the first principles (*principia*) proposed by each school with cumulative irony: Leucippus and Democritus postulate random collision of corpuscles of varying shapes; Thales, water; Anaximenes and Diogenes, air; Heraclitus and Hipparchus, fire; Empedocles, all four elements (identified with Jupiter, Pluto, Juno, and Nesti); Anaxagoras, infinite homogeneous bodies; Epicurus, eternal solid atoms with weight; Socrates and Plato, three principles — God, Matter, Idea; Aristotle, entelechy/species, matter, and privation (which Zorzi notes contradicts Aristotle's own teaching elsewhere about equivocal terms); Zeno, God and matter with the four elements intermediate; Diodorus Siculus, who confuses readers more than he instructs with his Osirian cosmogony.

Zorzi's decisive observation is that no materialist philosopher explains whether matter was created by God, was an eternal co-creatrix (*natura factibilis*), or was a coeternal spouse of Demogorgon, father of the gods. This lacuna is presented as the constitutive failure of pagan philosophy.

---

## Chapter 4: Philosophers Disagree on the Soul

The doxography of soul-theories (*de natura animae*) is the longest sub-section in the sample. Zorzi catalogs some thirty-plus positions: Anaximander (man evolved from fish-like beings in heated earth); Thales (matter similar to fire combined with earth); Pythagoreans divided between the motes in the air and the force moving them; Dicaearchus (a self-moving body); Anaximenes (air); Xenophanes (earth and water); Parmenides and Boetus (earth and fire); Epicurus (mixture of fire, air, and vital spirit in atomic sympathy, which Zorzi traces to the ancient Arab *Maurasphes* who admitted the spirit's entry into the soul but not its exit); Diogenes the Cynic (air); Empedocle (multiple elements); Anaxagoras (impassible, not subject to passion); Crates of Thebes (no soul at all); Democritus and Leucippus (spherical fire-shape); Hippocrates of Cos (fine breath diffused through the body); Asclepiades (flesh with the exercise of the five senses); Zeno (generated from divine fire, i.e. Providence); Galen (bodily constitution); Hipparchus (vigour of fire); Heraclitus of Ephesus (a spark of stellar essence); Heraclides Ponticus (light); Critolaus the Peripatetic (generated from quintessence); Posidonius (the idea); Chrysippus (spirit, since life and breathing are one). The Egyptians identify the soul as a power permeating all bodies; the Chaldeans as a potentiality without a fixed form.

Zorzi reserves particular criticism for Aristotle, whose definition of the soul as the perfection (*entelecheia*) of an organic body with life in potency is said to describe only the *effect* rather than the essence or origin of the soul — comparable, Zorzi quips, to defining the Pope or Emperor by saying he commands armies. Aristotle's deliberate obscurity on the soul's duration and immortality is what generated the Averroistic and Alexandrist bifurcation, both of which Zorzi finds maximally remote from piety and truth.

Pythagoras and Plato are treated with respect: the soul is a divine genus immersed in the bodily forest, having drunk Lethe. Their choice to conceal soul-doctrine under the veil of numbers is defended, citing Plato's own admission in *De Legibus* X that almost everyone has remained ignorant of what the soul truly is.

> «Pitagora ritenne che l'anima fosse una realtà di origine divina insita negli uomini, ricolma di forme ma immersa e quasi soffocata dal groviglio del corpo sì che pareva essere stata bagnata nelle acque del fiume Lete, cioè dell'oblio.»

<cite>p. 182 (chapter 4, Italian text)</cite>

Zorzi introduces here a four-level doctrine of number (*numero vocale, formale, razionale, divino*) — a technical Pythagorean-Neoplatonic hierarchy — arguing that those who did not understand this fourfold ascent fell into confusion about the soul.

---

## Chapter 5: Philosophers Disagree on the Final End

Zorzi invokes Augustine's report (drawn from Marcus Varro) that 288 views on the *finis* of human life were catalogued, and Plotinus's treatise *On the One and the Good* (*Enneads* I.7), which lists eight. The summary is rapid: Stoics (virtuous effort); Epicureans (pleasure); Peripatetics (speculation); Platonists (union with the supreme Good). The Platonic position is treated as the most consonant with Christianity: Plotinus teaches that one must recover the wings of a twofold philosophy to fly from the dark forest, mother of all evils, toward the mind through virtue, and toward God through wisdom.

---

## Technical Terms

- *Hypate, perhypate, lichanos, mese, paramese*: ancient Greek musical string-names used as cosmological-harmonic vocabulary
- *Endelecheia / entelechia*: Aristotle's term for the soul's defining activity
- *Omeomeri*: Anaxagoras's homoeomerous particles
- *Numero vocale / formale / razionale / divino*: Zorzi's four-tier numerical hierarchy (Pythagorean-Neoplatonic)
- *Maurasphes*: ancient Arab philosophical school mentioned regarding soul-doctrine
- *Interminata materia*: Anaximander's infinite material principle

---

## Connection to Zorzi's Larger Project

This chapter performs a key structural function in the *De Harmonia Mundi*: it establishes, by exhaustive negative doxography, why harmony cannot be achieved through secular philosophy alone. The One (*unum*) is the metaphysical condition of all consonance; departure from it produces the multiplicity and contradiction visible in the history of philosophy. Only the tradition rooted in divine revelation — Hebrew, then Christian — achieves genuine harmony because it is oriented toward the single arche. The chapter thus prepares the ground for Zorzi's positive synthesis, in which Kabbalah, Neoplatonism, Hermeticism, and Christian theology converge in a single *harmonia mundi*. The extended musical analogies — spheres, strings, angelic choirs, the scale of planetary tones — are not ornamental but structural to this argument.
